Pod traffic is restricted by having a Network Policy that selects it (through the use of labels). Once there is any Network Policy in a namespace selecting a particular pod, that pod will reject any connections that are not allowed by any Network Policy. Other pods in the namespace that are not selected by any Network Policy will continue to accept all traffic. Network Policies are managed via the Kubernetes Network Policy API and enforced by a network plugin, simply creating the resource without a compatible network plugin to implement it will have no effect. Impact: Network Policy requires the Network Policy add-on. This add-on is included automatically when a cluster with Network Policy is created, but for an existing cluster, needs to be added prior to enabling Network Policy. Enabling/Disabling Network Policy causes a rolling update of all cluster nodes, similar to performing a cluster upgrade. This operation is long-running and will block other operations on the cluster (including delete) until it has run to completion. Enabling Network Policy enforcement consumes additional resources in nodes. Specifically, it increases the memory footprint of the kube-system process by approximately 128MB, and requires approximately 300 millicores of CPU.
audit: Check for the following is not null and set with appropriate group id: export CLUSTER_NAME=<your cluster name> aws eks describe-cluster --name ${CLUSTER_NAME} --query "cluster.resourcesVpcConfig.clusterSecurityGroupId" Check for the following is True: export CLUSTER_NAME=<your cluster name> aws eks describe-addon --cluster-name ${CLUSTER_NAME} --addon-name vpc-cni -- query addon.configurationValues
remediation: Utilize Calico or other network policy engine to segment and isolate your traffic. Default Value: By default, Network Policy is disabled. References: 1. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/eks-networking-add-ons.html
impact: Network Policy requires the Network Policy add-on. This add-on is included automatically when a cluster with Network Policy is created, but for an existing cluster, needs to be added prior to enabling Network Policy. Enabling/Disabling Network Policy causes a rolling update of all cluster nodes, similar to performing a cluster upgrade. This operation is long-running and will block other operations on the cluster (including delete) until it has run to completion. Enabling Network Policy enforcement consumes additional resources in nodes. Specifically, it increases the memory footprint of the kube-system process by approximately 128MB, and requires approximately 300 millicores of CPU.
justification: While some aspects of EKS network configuration (such as the presence of the vpc-cni add-on and cluster security group) can be checked via AWS APIs, the core requirement of this control is to ensure that a Kubernetes NetworkPolicy is actually enabled and set appropriately within the cluster. AWS APIs (boto3, CLI) do not expose the presence or contents of Kubernetes NetworkPolicy resources, nor do they confirm if Calico or another network policy engine is actively enforcing policies at the pod level. This verification requires querying the Kubernetes API directly, which is not possible through AWS APIs alone and typically requires kubectl access to the cluster.
manual_summary: 1. **Obtain kubectl Access:** Ensure you have `kubectl` configured to access the target EKS cluster. 2. **Check for Network Policy Engine:** - Run `kubectl get pods -n kube-system` and look for Calico or other network policy engine pods (e.g., pods with names like `calico-node`). - Confirm that the network policy engine is running and healthy. 3. **Verify NetworkPolicy Resources:** - For each namespace, run: `kubectl get networkpolicy -n <namespace>` - Confirm that at least one NetworkPolicy exists in each namespace where pod-level traffic restriction is required. 4. **Review NetworkPolicy Configuration:** - For each NetworkPolicy, run: `kubectl describe networkpolicy <policy-name> -n <namespace>` - Ensure the policies are configured to restrict traffic as per your security requirements (e.g., default deny, allow only specific sources). 5. **Document Findings:** - Record which namespaces have NetworkPolicies, the policy names, and a summary of their rules. - Note any namespaces lacking policies or with overly permissive rules. 6. **Confirm Compliance:** - Compliance is achieved if a network policy engine is running and appropriate NetworkPolicy resources are present and configured to restrict traffic as required.

source: CIS AMAZON ELASTIC KUBERNETES SERVICE (EKS) BENCHMARK V1.6.0
title: Manage Kubernetes RBAC users with AWS IAM Authenticator for Kubernetes or Upgrade to AWS CLI v1.16.156 or greater
description: Amazon EKS uses IAM to provide authentication to your Kubernetes cluster through the AWS IAM Authenticator for Kubernetes. You can configure the stock kubectl client to work with Amazon EKS by installing the AWS IAM Authenticator for Kubernetes and modifying your kubectl configuration file to use it for authentication.
rationale: On- and off-boarding users is often difficult to automate and prone to error. Using a single source of truth for user permissions reduces the number of locations that an individual must be off-boarded from, and prevents users gaining unique permissions sets that increase the cost of audit. Impact: Users must now be assigned to the IAM group created to use this namespace and deploy applications. If they are not they will not be able to access the namespace or deploy.
audit: To Audit access to the namespace $NAMESPACE, assume the IAM role yourIAMRoleName for a user that you created, and then run the following command: $ kubectl get role -n $NAMESPACE The response lists the RBAC role that has access to this Namespace.
remediation: Refer to the 'Managing users or IAM roles for your cluster' in Amazon EKS documentation. Note: If using AWS CLI version 1.16.156 or later there is no need to install the AWS IAM Authenticator anymore. The relevant AWS CLI commands, depending on the use case, are: aws eks update-kubeconfig aws eks get-token Default Value: For role-based access control (RBAC), system:masters permissions are configured in the Amazon EKS control plane References: 1. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/add-user-role.html 2. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/add-user-role.html
impact: Users must now be assigned to the IAM group created to use this namespace and deploy applications. If they are not they will not be able to access the namespace or deploy.
justification: While aspects of EKS IAM authentication configuration can be verified using AWS APIs (such as confirming cluster IAM roles and authentication mappings via boto3 and the AWS CLI), a critical part of this control is ensuring that the AWS CLI version in use is 1.16.156 or greater. The AWS CLI version is an attribute of the client environment, not an AWS-managed resource, and cannot be programmatically checked via AWS APIs or SDKs from a remote perspective. Therefore, full compliance with this control requires manual verification of the CLI version on all relevant operator workstations, in addition to reviewing EKS IAM authentication configuration.
manual_summary: 1. **Verify AWS CLI Version on Operator Workstations:** - On each workstation or CI/CD runner that interacts with the EKS cluster, run `aws --version`. - Confirm the output shows AWS CLI version 1.16.156 or greater. 2. **Check EKS Authentication Configuration:** - Use the AWS Console or CLI (`aws eks describe-cluster --name <cluster_name>`) to review the cluster's authentication configuration. - Ensure that IAM roles/users are mapped correctly in the `aws-auth` ConfigMap within the EKS cluster (`kubectl -n kube-system get configmap aws-auth -o yaml`). 3. **Test Access:** - Assume the relevant IAM role and attempt to run `kubectl get role -n $NAMESPACE` to confirm that RBAC is enforced as expected. 4. **Document Findings:** - Record the AWS CLI version(s) in use and capture evidence of correct IAM role mappings and RBAC enforcement.

source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure MFA is enabled for WorkSpaces users
justification: While the first opinion suggests automation is possible via AWS Directory Service APIs, this is technically inaccurate. The AWS Directory Service API does not expose per-user MFA status; MFA for WorkSpaces users is typically enforced via integration with Active Directory or a third-party IdP, and AWS APIs do not provide a method to enumerate or verify individual user MFA status. Therefore, verification requires manual inspection of the directory's MFA configuration and/or the IdP settings.
manual_summary: 1. Identify the directory (Simple AD, AD Connector, or AWS Managed Microsoft AD) associated with your AWS WorkSpaces deployment via the AWS Console: WorkSpaces > Directories. 2. Determine the authentication method in use: - If using AWS Managed Microsoft AD or AD Connector, MFA is typically enforced via RADIUS or integration with an IdP. - If using Simple AD, review the documentation for supported MFA options. 3. For RADIUS-based MFA: - In the AWS Console, go to Directory Service > Directories > [Your Directory] > Multi-Factor Authentication. - Confirm that MFA is enabled and configured (e.g., RADIUS server details are present and status is "Enabled"). 4. For SAML/IdP-based MFA: - Access your IdP (e.g., Azure AD, Okta, ADFS) management console. - Verify that MFA is enforced for all users/groups associated with AWS WorkSpaces. 5. Document the MFA configuration and capture evidence (screenshots or configuration exports) showing MFA enforcement. 6. Confirm compliance by ensuring all users who can access AWS WorkSpaces are subject to MFA at login.

source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure access is limited to trusted devices
justification: While AWS services like IAM and AWS Config can enforce and monitor some access restrictions (e.g., source IPs, VPC endpoints), the core requirement of this control—ensuring access is limited to "trusted devices"—inherently involves device posture, endpoint security, and device management policies that are not exposed or verifiable via AWS APIs. AWS does not natively provide mechanisms to determine or validate device trust status (such as MDM enrollment, endpoint compliance, or device certificates) at the point of access. Therefore, full compliance with this control requires manual verification of organizational device management and access policies.
manual_summary: 1. **Review Organizational Policies:** Obtain and review the organization's security policies to confirm there are documented requirements for restricting AWS access to trusted devices (e.g., devices enrolled in MDM, with up-to-date security patches, and endpoint protection). 2. **Verify Device Management Implementation:** Confirm that a device management solution (such as MDM or endpoint security platform) is in place and actively used to enforce device trust requirements. 3. **Check Access Enforcement:** Review how access to AWS resources is technically enforced (e.g., integration with SSO providers that support device posture checks, conditional access policies, or network-based restrictions). 4. **Sample Validation:** Select a sample of users and verify, through device management logs or attestation, that only trusted devices are permitted to access AWS resources. 5. **Document Evidence:** Collect evidence of device management enforcement and access controls for audit purposes. 6. **Confirm No AWS-Native Automation:** Acknowledge that AWS APIs, IAM, or Config cannot directly validate device trust status; enforcement and verification must be performed outside AWS.

source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure your WorkSpaces image has the appropriate CIS Operating System Benchmark applied
references: 1. https://www.cisecurity.org/partners-vendor/ 2. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/workspaces/latest/adminguide/create-custom- bundle.html 3. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/workspaces/latest/adminguide/update-custom- bundle.html CIS Controls: Controls Version Control IG 1 IG 2 IG 3 v8 4.1 Establish and Maintain a Secure Configuration Process Establish and maintain a secure configuration process for enterprise assets (end-user devices, including portable and mobile, non-computing/IoT devices, and servers) and software (operating systems and applications). Review and update documentation annually, or when significant enterprise changes occur that could impact this Safeguard. ● ● ● v7 5.1 Establish Secure Configurations Maintain documented, standard security configuration standards for all authorized operating systems and software. ● ● ●
justification: Verifying that a WorkSpaces image has the appropriate CIS Operating System Benchmark applied requires inspection of the internal OS configuration within the image. AWS APIs, including boto3, AWS CLI, and AWS Config, do not expose the necessary OS-level configuration details for WorkSpaces images. While AWS Systems Manager could potentially be used to run compliance scripts on active WorkSpaces, it cannot directly validate the baseline of a custom image or bundle itself without manual intervention. Therefore, this control cannot be fully automated using AWS-native API capabilities.
manual_summary: 1. Launch a WorkSpace using the custom image or bundle under review. 2. Connect to the WorkSpace instance via RDP (for Windows) or the appropriate method for the OS. 3. Use a CIS Benchmark assessment tool (such as CIS-CAT or a relevant script) to scan the WorkSpace for compliance with the applicable CIS Operating System Benchmark. 4. Review the assessment report to confirm that all required CIS controls are applied and compliant. 5. Document the results and retain evidence (e.g., screenshots, reports) for audit purposes.

source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Restrict WorkSpaces Bundle options to organization approved versions
remediation: Preform the following to limit the bundle type Create the required AWS support case: From the Console: 1. Login in to AWS Support Center dashboard at https://console.aws.amazon.com/support/. 2. Click Create a case. 3. For Case details: - Type, choose `Account` - Category, choose `Other Account Issues` - Subject, "Limit AWS WorkSpaces instances launch to approved bundle types". - Description textbox, explain that security and compliance requires the need to limit the provisioning of WorkSpaces instances to an approved bundle type. - Contact options, leave as default or change as needed.  4. Click Submit Default Value: By default, there is no bundle restriction. This is a manual decision that must be made by technology stakeholders in your organization. References: 1. https://aws.amazon.com/workspaces/faqs/ 2. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/workspaces/latest/adminguide/amazon- workspaces-bundles.html 3. https://aws.amazon.com/workspaces/features/ 4. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_polici es_scps.html CIS Controls:
justification: While AWS Config or custom scripts could potentially monitor existing WorkSpaces for compliance with approved bundle types, there is no AWS API, CLI, or direct automation method to restrict or enforce which WorkSpaces bundles are available for provisioning. The actual enforcement of bundle restrictions requires submitting a support case to AWS, as described in the remediation steps, which is inherently a manual process. Automated monitoring can only alert, not enforce, and does not fulfill the intent of this control.
manual_summary: 1. Log in to the AWS Support Center at https://console.aws.amazon.com/support/. 2. Click "Create a case." 3. For Case details: - Type: Select "Account." - Category: Select "Other Account Issues." - Subject: Enter "Limit AWS WorkSpaces instances launch to approved bundle types." - In the Description textbox, clearly explain that security and compliance require limiting the provisioning of WorkSpaces to only organization-approved bundle types. List the approved bundle types. 4. Submit the case and retain the case ID for audit purposes. 5. After AWS implements the restriction, attempt to launch a WorkSpace with a non-approved bundle to confirm the restriction is enforced. 6. Document the outcome and retain evidence of the restriction for compliance verification.

source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure Radius server is using the recommended security protocol
remediation: Perform the steps below to set the protocol to MS-CHAPv2 for multi-factor authentication. From the console: For AWS Managed AD based environments; 1. Log in to the Directory Service console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/directoryservicev2 2. In the left pane select Directories. 3. On the Directory details page, select the Networking & security tab. 4. In the Multi-factor authentication section, choose Actions, and then choose Edit. 5. On the Enable multi-factor authentication (MFA) page change the following value: 6. Protocol - MS-CHAPv2 7. Click Save. For directory connector / self-managed AD environments 1. Log in to the AWS Workspaces console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/workspaces 2. In the left pane select Directories. 3. Select the directory ID link for your AWS Managed Microsoft AD directory. 4. On the Directories page, scroll to the Multi-factor authentication section and select Edit. 5. In the Multi-factor authentication section modify the protocol using the dropdown menu to be MS-CHAPv2 from the currently selected option. 6. Click Save once settings are as desired. Default Value: By default, this is dependent on your RADIUS server. References: 1. https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/how-to-enable-multi-factor-authentication- for-amazon-workspaces-and-amazon-quicksight-by-using-microsoft-ad-and-on- premises-credentials/ 2. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/directoryservice/latest/admin- guide/ms_ad_mfa.html CIS Controls:
justification: Both AI opinions correctly identify that AWS does not currently expose the RADIUS server security protocol configuration (specifically MS-CHAPv2 for MFA) via any public API, AWS CLI, or SDK. This setting is only accessible and configurable through the AWS Management Console UI for Directory Service and WorkSpaces. There is no AWS Config rule or resource property that can be queried or monitored for this protocol setting, making full automation impossible at this time.
manual_summary: 1. Log in to the AWS Directory Service console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/directoryservicev2. 2. In the left pane, select "Directories." 3. Click on the relevant Directory. On the Directory details page, select the "Networking & security" tab. 4. In the "Multi-factor authentication" section, choose "Actions," then "Edit." 5. On the "Enable multi-factor authentication (MFA)" page, verify that the "Protocol" is set to "MS-CHAPv2." 6. If not set, change the protocol to "MS-CHAPv2" and save the configuration. 7. Confirm that the protocol change has been saved and is reflected in the configuration. 8. Document the setting (screenshot or note) for audit evidence.

source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure MFA is enabled for WorkDoc users
justification: While the first opinion suggests automation via IAM APIs, the second opinion correctly points out that Amazon WorkDocs users are not always managed as IAM users; they may be provisioned via AWS Directory Service, SAML, or other identity providers. AWS APIs (including boto3 and AWS CLI) do not provide a direct method to enumerate all WorkDocs users and check their MFA status across all possible identity sources. Therefore, a reliable, comprehensive automated check is not feasible.
manual_summary: 1. Log in to the AWS Management Console as an administrator. 2. Navigate to the Amazon WorkDocs console. 3. Select the relevant WorkDocs site. 4. Review the user directory to identify all active WorkDocs users. 5. Determine the identity provider in use (AWS Directory Service, SAML, or IAM). 6. For AWS Directory Service or SAML, access the respective identity provider’s management console (e.g., Microsoft Active Directory, Okta, ADFS). 7. Verify that MFA is enforced for all users who have access to WorkDocs, according to the identity provider’s MFA policy settings. 8. Document the MFA enforcement policy and provide evidence (e.g., screenshots or policy exports) that MFA is enabled for all WorkDocs users. 9. Confirm compliance by ensuring that all user accounts require MFA at login.

source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure Workdocs access is limited to a range of allowable IP addresses
justification: AWS WorkDocs does not currently expose IP restriction settings via AWS APIs, SDKs (such as boto3), AWS CLI, or AWS Config. The configuration for allowable IP addresses is managed within the WorkDocs administrative console and is not accessible for direct programmatic inspection or enforcement. Both AI opinions correctly note the lack of automation capability, with the second opinion suggesting indirect monitoring via AWS Config or CloudTrail; however, these services do not provide visibility into the specific IP restriction settings required by this control.
manual_summary: 1. Log in to the AWS Management Console and navigate to the WorkDocs service. 2. Select the relevant WorkDocs site and access the administrative settings. 3. Locate the section for network or access control settings (often under "Site settings" or "Access controls"). 4. Review the configuration for IP address restrictions and verify that only the approved range(s) of IP addresses are permitted. 5. Confirm that the configuration matches organizational policy and that no unauthorized IP ranges are allowed. 6. Document your findings, including screenshots or configuration details as evidence of compliance.

source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure new users can only be invited from allowed domains.
justification: While some directory and user information can be queried via AWS APIs (such as with WorkSpaces or Directory Service), the specific configuration for restricting user invitations to allowed email domains is not exposed through AWS APIs, CLI, or SDKs. This setting is typically managed within the AWS Management Console or external identity providers and is not programmatically retrievable or verifiable. Therefore, a manual review of the relevant console settings is required to ensure compliance.
manual_summary: 1. Log in to the AWS Management Console. 2. Navigate to the relevant End User Compute service (e.g., AWS WorkSpaces, AppStream 2.0, or the specific service in use). 3. Access the user management or directory settings section (often under "Directories," "User Invitations," or "Identity Providers"). 4. Locate the configuration for allowed domains or domain restrictions for user invitations. 5. Review the list of allowed domains and verify that only approved domains (as per organizational policy) are present. 6. Document the allowed domains and compare them against the organization's approved list. 7. Confirm that no unauthorized domains are permitted for user invitations.

source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure session Idle disconnect timeout is set to 10 minutes or less
justification: While the first AI opinion suggests automation via boto3, the second opinion correctly points out that the session idle disconnect timeout for AWS WorkSpaces is not directly accessible through any current AWS API, CLI, or SDK method. This setting is managed via the AWS Console under WorkSpaces client properties and is not exposed programmatically for audit. Therefore, verification must be performed manually through the AWS Console.
manual_summary: 1. Log in to the AWS Management Console and navigate to the Amazon WorkSpaces service. 2. In the left navigation pane, select "Directories." 3. For each directory, click on the directory name to open its details. 4. Under the "Access Control Options" or "User Access" section, locate the "Client Properties" or "Session Settings." 5. Verify that the "Idle Disconnect Timeout" is set to 10 minutes (600 seconds) or less. 6. Repeat this process for all directories and WorkSpaces in your environment. 7. Document your findings, including screenshots or configuration details as evidence of compliance.

source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure Operating system updates are applied to your base image every 30 days.
justification: Verifying that operating system updates are applied to base images every 30 days requires inspecting the actual contents and patch level of the AMI or golden image. AWS APIs (boto3, AWS CLI) do not expose OS-level update metadata for AMIs, nor do they provide a direct method to confirm the patch status of an image. While AWS Systems Manager and Inspector can automate checks on running instances, they cannot retroactively verify the update status of the AMI itself without launching and inspecting it, which is outside the scope of a pure API-based audit.
manual_summary: 1. Identify the base AMIs used for launching EC2 instances or other compute resources. 2. For each base AMI, launch a temporary EC2 instance using the image. 3. SSH into the instance (using appropriate credentials and security group settings). 4. Run the OS-specific command to list installed updates and check the last update date (e.g., `yum history`, `apt list --upgradable`, `rpm -qa --last`, etc.). 5. Verify that all critical and security updates have been applied within the last 30 days. 6. Document the AMI ID, the date of the last applied update, and the verification result. 7. Terminate the temporary instance after verification. 8. Repeat this process for all base images in use.

source: CIS AWS STORAGE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1
title: Ensure securing AWS Backups
description: As an AWS administrator, it's important to know what you're responsible for. You're responsible for keeping things safe in the cloud, which means taking care of the resources and data on AWS. Here's what you need to secure, according to AWS documentation: 1. Responsible for alert communication with AWS. 2. Managing access credentials for AWS resources. 3. Configuring backup plans according to organization policies. 4. Ensuring backup recovery capability. 5. Including AWS Backups in the organization's disaster recovery procedures. 6. Ensuring user awareness and familiarity with AWS Backups platform usage
rationale: AWS will send periodic emails regarding the status of your backups and any service issues. The administrator must address any communicated issues from AWS, such as billing problems or backup inactivity, and take necessary steps to resolve them.
audit: CREATING AN AWS BACKUP: Creating an AWS Backup involves selecting the desired data, specifying backup frequency, and choosing storage options. Below we’ll walk through how to create and configure an AWS Backup instance. 1. Sign into AWS Console: To sign into the AWS Console 'https://console.aws.amazon.com/billing/home#/', users navigate to the AWS Management Console website and enter their credentials, including their username and password. 2. Access the AWS Backup Service Dashboard in the AWS Management Console: AWS Management Console and type "Backup" or navigate through the services menu to find the "Storage" category, where AWS Backup is listed. 3. Create Backup Plan: Choose "Create backup plan" from the options provided. You can either create a custom plan tailored to your requirements or option for a per-defined template offered by AWS
remediation: References: 1. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/aws-backup/latest/devguide/whatisbackup.html
justification: While certain technical aspects of AWS Backup configuration—such as the existence and settings of backup plans, recovery points, and alerting mechanisms—can be verified using AWS APIs (boto3, CLI), the full scope of this control explicitly requires validation of organizational policy alignment, administrator awareness, and integration into disaster recovery (DR) procedures. These elements inherently require human judgment, cross-team communication, and review of documentation and processes that are not exposed or verifiable via AWS APIs. Therefore, a fully automated approach cannot satisfy the intent and requirements of this control.
manual_summary: 1. **Review Backup Policies and Plans:** - Sign in to the AWS Management Console. - Navigate to the AWS Backup service. - Review all configured backup plans and confirm they align with organizational policy (frequency, retention, recovery point objectives). 2. **Verify Alert Communication:** - Check the contact information and alerting mechanisms (e.g., email, SNS topics) configured for AWS Backup notifications. - Confirm that responsible administrators are receiving and responding to AWS communications regarding backup status and issues. 3. **Assess Access Management:** - Review IAM roles and policies associated with AWS Backup to ensure only authorized personnel can manage backup resources. 4. **Check DR Integration:** - Obtain and review the organization’s disaster recovery (DR) plan. - Confirm that AWS Backup resources and recovery procedures are explicitly included and tested within the DR plan. 5. **Validate Recovery Capability:** - Select a recent backup and perform a test restore (or review logs of recent test restores) to ensure recovery capability is functional. 6. **Document Findings:** - Record evidence of each step, including screenshots, policy documents, and test results.

source: CIS AWS STORAGE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1
title: Ensure the proper configuration of EBS storage
description: All computer instances need to have a device on which to store files. EBS is built on top of EC2 instances as a block storage device.
rationale: Remember that we are working with cloud computing. Rather than purchasing and manually installing disk drives on a server, AWS allows you to virtually add storage using Elastic Block Store (EBS). Impact: Failure to properly configure EBS storage can lead to data loss, performance issues, increased costs, security vulnerabilities, and operational downtime. Ensuring correct configuration is crucial to maintain data integrity, efficiency, cost-effectiveness, security, and reliability.
remediation: 1. Open the Amazon EC2 Console : Navigate to the EC2 Dashboard in the AWS Management Console. 2. Select Volumes : Under the "Elastic Block Store" section, select "Volumes". 3. Create Volume : o Click on "Create Volume". o Choose the volume type (e.g., General Purpose SSD (gp2), Provisioned IOPS SSD (io1), etc.). o Specify the size and availability zone. o Optionally, configure additional settings such as IOPS, encryption, and tags. 4. Attach Volume to Instance : o Select the volume you created. o Click on "Actions" and choose "Attach Volume". o Select the instance to which you want to attach the volume and specify the device name. 5. Format and Mount the Volume (on the instance): o Connect to your instance using SSH. o List available disks using the command: lsblk. o Format the new volume (e.g., sudo mkfs -t ext4 /dev/xvdf for ext4 filesystem). o Create a mount point (e.g., sudo mkdir /mnt/data). o Mount the volume (e.g., sudo mount /dev/xvdf /mnt/data). 6. Configure Automatic Mounting (optional) : o Edit the /etc/fstab file to add an entry for the new volume to ensure it mounts automatically on reboot. o Example entry: /dev/xvdf /mnt/data ext4 defaults,nofail 0 2. By following these steps, you can effectively configure and manage EBS storage for your AWS instances.
impact: Failure to properly configure EBS storage can lead to data loss, performance issues, increased costs, security vulnerabilities, and operational downtime. Ensuring correct configuration is crucial to maintain data integrity, efficiency, cost-effectiveness, security, and reliability.
justification: While some aspects of EBS configuration (such as verifying that volumes are attached to EC2 instances) can be automated using AWS APIs, the core of this control—ensuring proper configuration of EBS storage—explicitly requires verification of OS-level actions such as formatting, mounting, and `/etc/fstab` configuration. These steps are not accessible via AWS APIs or Config rules and require SSH access to the instance for validation. Therefore, a fully automated audit is not possible for this control.
manual_summary: 1. **Verify EBS Volume Attachment:** - In the AWS Management Console, navigate to EC2 > Instances. - Select the target instance and review the "Block devices" section to confirm the correct EBS volumes are attached. 2. **SSH into the Instance:** - Use SSH to access the EC2 instance with the attached EBS volume. 3. **Check Device Recognition:** - Run `lsblk` or `fdisk -l` to ensure the EBS volume is recognized by the operating system. 4. **Verify Formatting:** - Run `sudo file -s /dev/xvdf` (replace `/dev/xvdf` with the correct device) to confirm the volume is formatted with an appropriate filesystem (e.g., ext4, xfs). 5. **Check Mount Point:** - Run `mount` or `df -h` to verify the volume is mounted at the intended directory. 6. **Confirm Auto-Mount Configuration:** - Inspect `/etc/fstab` to ensure the EBS volume is configured to mount automatically on reboot with correct options. 7. **(Optional) Reboot Test:** - Reboot the instance and confirm the EBS volume mounts as expected.

source: CIS AWS STORAGE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1
title: EFS
description: AWS EFS is a scalable and fully-managed storage service that enables you to quickly deploy file systems without the hassle of configuring, patching, or maintaining them.
rationale: Utilize AWS EFS to streamline your file system deployment, allowing the service to handle the heavy lifting for you. Impact: Not using AWS EFS for your file system deployment can lead to increased management overhead, as you'll need to manually configure, patch, and maintain the systems. This manual effort is time-consuming and complex, raising the potential for errors that could result in downtime and data loss. By not leveraging AWS EFS, you miss out on the streamlined, automated management and scalability that the service provides, potentially impacting your operational efficiency and reliability.
audit: To create an Amazon EFS (Elastic File System), you can follow these steps: 1. Sign in to the AWS Management Console and navigate to the Amazon EFS console - https://us-east-2.console.aws.amazon.com/efs?region=us-east-2#/get- started. 2. Click on the "Create file system" button. 3. Enter a name for your file system. 4. Choose a VPC for your file system 5. Then you have to go to File system settings to edit configurations , then you have to select Lifecycle management , performance settings and File system protection , and then click save changes.
remediation: To create an Amazon EFS (Elastic File System), follow these steps: 1. Open the Amazon EFS Console : Sign in to your AWS Management Console and navigate to the Amazon EFS service. 2. Create File System : Click on the "Create file system" button to start the creation process. 3. Configure File System : Select your desired VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) and availability zones for the file system. Optionally, you can configure settings like throughput mode and lifecycle management. 4. Configure Access Points : Set up access points if needed, to control access permissions and streamline access management. 5. Review and Create : Review your settings and click on the "Create" button to create the file system. 6. Mount the File System : Once created, use the provided mount targets and instructions to mount the file system to your EC2 instances or other resources. References: 1. https://us-east-2.console.aws.amazon.com/efs?region=us-east-2#/get-started
impact: Not using AWS EFS for your file system deployment can lead to increased management overhead, as you'll need to manually configure, patch, and maintain the systems. This manual effort is time-consuming and complex, raising the potential for errors that could result in downtime and data loss. By not leveraging AWS EFS, you miss out on the streamlined, automated management and scalability that the service provides, potentially impacting your operational efficiency and reliability.
justification: While AWS APIs (boto3, CLI) can automate checks for specific EFS security configurations (e.g., encryption, access policies), this control is fundamentally about the organizational decision to use EFS for file system deployment, not about verifying a technical security property of EFS itself. The audit and remediation steps focus on confirming that EFS is being used, which is a business/architectural choice and cannot be programmatically verified through AWS APIs. There is no API call that can determine whether EFS is being used "in place of" other file system solutions across an organization.
manual_summary: 1. Log in to the AWS Management Console. 2. Navigate to the Amazon EFS console. 3. Review all existing file systems and confirm that EFS is being used for organizational file storage needs. 4. Interview system owners or review architectural documentation to verify that EFS is the standard for file system deployment, rather than self-managed EC2-based file servers or other unmanaged solutions. 5. Document evidence (screenshots, architecture diagrams, meeting notes) showing that EFS is in use for relevant workloads. 6. Confirm that the organization’s policy or standard operating procedure mandates the use of EFS for new file system deployments.

source: CIS AWS STORAGE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1
title: Amazon Elastic File Cache
description: Amazon File Cache is available in the following AWS Regions: 1. US East (N. Virginia) 2. US East (Ohio) 3. US West (Oregon) 4. Canada (Central) 5. Europe (Frankfurt) 6. Europe (Ireland) 7. Europe (London) 8. Europe (Stockholm) 9. Asia Pacific (Hong Kong) 10. Asia Pacific (Mumbai) 11. Asia Pacific (Seoul) 12. Asia Pacific (Tokyo) 13. Asia Pacific (Singapore) 14. Asia Pacific (Sydney) Amazon Elastic File Cache Compatibility: In order to use AWS FSx, you must ensure that the operating system you’re using on the compute instance is compatible with AWS FSx. Below are the compatible operating systems: 1. Amazon Linux 2 and Amazon Linux 2. Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) 3. CentOS 4. Rocky Linux 5. Ubuntu. The Lustre client must be installed on these systems in order for the FSx service to work.
rationale: The rationale behind creating Amazon Elastic File Cache is to enhance the performance and scalability of cloud-based applications by providing a high-speed, scalable file caching solution. This service reduces latency and improves access times for frequently accessed data, thereby optimizing application performance and user experience. Additionally, it helps manage and reduce storage costs by efficiently utilizing cached data, ensuring that resources are used effectively while maintaining high performance standards. Impact: Not implementing Amazon Elastic File Cache can lead to increased latency and slower access times for frequently accessed data, resulting in suboptimal performance for cloud-based applications. This can negatively affect user experience and productivity. Additionally, without an efficient caching solution, there may be higher storage costs due to inefficient use of resources, and the system may struggle to handle high demand, leading to potential performance bottlenecks and scalability issues.
audit: Creating Amazon Elastic File Cache: Before you can start using Amazon Elastic File Cache, you must set up an Amazon Elastic Compute Instance and an S3 bucket. We’re going to create a new EC2 instance and S3 bucket for the sake of this tutorial. Creating an EC2 instance for FSx: Make sure that whatever AMI you select is compatible with Lustre 2.12 client. - Navigate to the Amazon EC2 console. - Select “Launch Instance”. - Give your server a name. - Select “Ubuntu” or an operating system that’s compatible with FSx. - Select default VPC and security group. - Select or create private SSH keys. - Leave the rest of the settings default. - Create Instance.
remediation: References: 1. https://aws.amazon.com/fsx/
impact: Not implementing Amazon Elastic File Cache can lead to increased latency and slower access times for frequently accessed data, resulting in suboptimal performance for cloud-based applications. This can negatively affect user experience and productivity. Additionally, without an efficient caching solution, there may be higher storage costs due to inefficient use of resources, and the system may struggle to handle high demand, leading to potential performance bottlenecks and scalability issues.
justification: While some aspects of the control—such as verifying the AMI used for EC2 instances—can be partially automated via AWS APIs, the core compliance requirement is to ensure the operating system is compatible with the Lustre client and that the Lustre client is actually installed and configured. This requires SSH access to the EC2 instance and OS-level inspection, which cannot be performed through AWS APIs alone. Therefore, full compliance verification cannot be automated.
manual_summary: 1. **Verify EC2 Instance Setup:** - Log in to the AWS Management Console. - Navigate to the EC2 dashboard and identify all instances intended for use with Amazon FSx. - Review the AMI (Amazon Machine Image) used for each instance and ensure it is one of the supported operating systems for the Lustre client (e.g., Amazon Linux 2, Ubuntu, RHEL, CentOS, Rocky Linux). 2. **Confirm Lustre Client Installation:** - SSH into each EC2 instance identified in Step 1. - Run the appropriate command for the OS (e.g., `rpm -qa | grep lustre` for RPM-based, `dpkg -l | grep lustre` for Debian-based) to verify the Lustre client is installed. - Confirm the installed version matches the required version for compatibility (e.g., Lustre 2.12). 3. **Validate Configuration:** - Check that the Lustre client is properly configured to connect to the FSx file system. - Optionally, attempt to mount the FSx file system and verify successful access. 4. **Document Evidence:** - Take screenshots or export command outputs showing the OS version, Lustre client installation, and successful mount/configuration.

source: CIS AWS STORAGE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1
title: Ensure installation and configuration of Lustre Client
description: To utilize the newly created File Cache, you must install the Lustre Client on your EC2 instance.
rationale: The Lustre Client facilitates efficient communication between the EC2 instance and the File Cache, ensuring high-performance data access and improved overall system efficiency. This setup is crucial for optimizing data processing and leveraging the benefits of the File Cache.
audit: Follow along to install the Lustre Client on Ubuntu 22.04: 1. Launch your EC2 instance. Navigate to the folder of your secure key and ssh into the instance using this command: - ssh -i "{KEY.pem}" ubuntu@{your ec2 instance} - When prompted to log in with the SSH key, enter in “yes” - You should now be connected to your EC2 instance. 2. Run the following command to download and install the public Lustre key: wget -O - https://fsx-lustre-client-repo-public-keys.s3.amazonaws.com/fsx- ubuntu-public-key.asc | gpg --dearmor | sudo tee /usr/share/keyrings/fsx- ubuntu-public-key.gpg >/dev/null  3. Add the AWS Lustre package repository to your local package manager using the following command: sudo bash -c 'echo "deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/fsx-ubuntu-public- key.gpg] https://fsx-lustre-client-repo.s3.amazonaws.com/ubuntu jammy main" > /etc/apt/sources.list.d/fsxlustreclientrepo.list && apt-get update'  4. Determine which kernel is currently running on your client instance and update as needed. The AWS Lustre client on Ubuntu 22.02 requires kernel 5.15.0.1020- aws or later for both x86 based EC2 instances and Arm-based EC2 instanced powered by AWS Graviton processors: a. Run the following command to find out which kernel your machine is running: uname -r o If your kernel is not up to date, run the following command: This will install the kernel update, Lustre client update, as well as reboot your system. sudo apt install -y linux-aws lustre-client-modules-aws && sudo reboot - If your kernel is up to date and you just want to install the latest Lustre version, run this command: sudo apt install -y lustre-client-modules-$(uname -r)
justification: While some aspects of installing the Lustre Client on EC2 instances could theoretically be automated using AWS Systems Manager (SSM), the compliance control specifically requires OS-level configuration and verification steps (such as SSH access, package installation, and keyring verification) that are not directly auditable via AWS APIs, boto3, or AWS Config. The installation process is inherently tied to the guest OS and not exposed through AWS-native APIs, making full automation and verification via AWS tools infeasible for compliance audit purposes.
manual_summary: 1. **SSH into the EC2 Instance:** - Open your terminal and navigate to the directory containing your SSH private key (e.g., KEY.pem). - Connect to your EC2 instance using: ``` ssh -i "KEY.pem" ubuntu@<EC2_INSTANCE_PUBLIC_IP> ``` - Type “yes” if prompted to confirm the connection. 2. **Download and Install the Lustre Client Public Key:** - Run: ``` wget -O - https://fsx-lustre-client-repo-public-keys.s3.amazonaws.com/fsx-ubuntu-public-key.asc | gpg --dearmor | sudo tee /usr/share/keyrings/fsx-ubuntu-public-key.gpg >/dev/null ``` - Confirm the key is present in `/usr/share/keyrings/`. 3. **Add the Lustre Package Repository:** - Run: ``` echo "deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/fsx-ubuntu-public-key.gpg] https://fsx-lustre-client-repo.s3.amazonaws.com/ubuntu jammy main" | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/fsx-lustre-client.list ``` 4. **Update Package List and Install Lustre Client:** - Run: ``` sudo apt-get update sudo apt-get install -y lustre-client-modules-$(uname -r) lustre-client-utils ``` 5. **Verify Installation:** - Check that the Lustre kernel modules are loaded: ``` lsmod | grep lustre ``` - Confirm the Lustre client utilities are installed: ``` which lfs ``` 6. **Document Compliance:** - Take screenshots or logs of the above steps and outputs for audit records.

source: CIS AWS STORAGE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1
title: Ensure EC2 Kernel compatibility with Lustre
description: The latest kernel included with the Ubuntu Amazon EC2 AMI is not compatible with the Lustre service, which is crucial for mounting the cache on your EC2 instance. To downgrade your kernel, specific prerequisites must be met if you are using the default Ubuntu machine image as of November 8, 2023.
rationale: The latest kernel version is not supported by Lustre, and meeting the prerequisites for downgrading will allow you to leverage Lustre's high-performance file system capabilities effectively. This ensures optimal data access and processing efficiency on your EC2 instance.
audit: Follow the steps to downgrade your kernel: 1. List all of the available Lustre packages by typing in this command: sudo apt- cache search lustre-client-modules. This will show a list of supported modules with corresponding kernel versions in ascending order from top to bottom. The most recent version in this case is “lustre-client-modules-5.15.0-1049-aws. Save this information for the next commands. 2. Install the most recent linux image that supports the Lustre client with this command: sudo apt-get install -y linux-image-5.15.0-1049-aws sudo sed -i 's/GRUB_DEFAULT=.\+/GRUB\_DEFAULT="Advanced options for Ubuntu>Ubuntu, with Linux 5.15.0-1049-aws"/' /etc/default/grub  3. Reboot your system by typing “sudo reboot”. 4. Install the correct Lustre module: . sudo apt-get install -y lustre-client-modules-$(uname -r)
remediation: References: 1. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/fsx/latest/LustreGuide/install-lustre-client.html
justification: The control requires verification and potential modification of the EC2 instance's operating system kernel to ensure compatibility with Lustre. This involves running OS-level commands (e.g., uname -r, apt-cache search) and potentially installing a specific kernel version, none of which can be performed or verified directly via AWS APIs, boto3, AWS CLI, or AWS Config. While some automation is possible with configuration management tools, the compliance check itself cannot be fully automated using AWS-native services or APIs.
manual_summary: 1. **Access the EC2 Instance:** - SSH into the target EC2 instance using appropriate credentials. 2. **Check Current Kernel Version:** - Run `uname -r` to display the current kernel version. 3. **List Available Lustre-Compatible Kernel Modules:** - Execute `sudo apt-cache search lustre-client-modules` to list all available Lustre client modules and their corresponding kernel versions. 4. **Verify Compatibility:** - Compare the current kernel version with the list of supported versions for Lustre. Confirm if the current kernel is compatible. 5. **Remediate if Necessary:** - If the kernel is not compatible, install a supported kernel version and the corresponding Lustre client module as per AWS documentation. 6. **Document Compliance:** - Record the kernel version and the installed Lustre client module for audit evidence.

source: CIS AWS STORAGE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1
title: Ensure mounting FSx cache
description: Mounting the FSx cache is a crucial step to optimize data retrieval and system performance. This process involves connecting the FSx file system to your compute instances, allowing them to access cached data efficiently. Properly mounting the FSx cache ensures low-latency access to frequently used data, enhances overall application performance, and leverages the full capabilities of the AWS FSx service. This setup is essential for achieving high performance and efficient data processing in your AWS environment.
rationale: By connecting the FSx file system to your compute instances, you enable low-latency access to frequently used data, significantly improving application performance. This setup leverages the full capabilities of the AWS FSx service, ensuring efficient data processing and resource utilization in your AWS environment. Properly mounting the FSx cache is essential for achieving high performance and operational efficiency.
audit: To mount your cache, follow the next steps: 1. Make a directory for the mount point with the following command: sudo mkdir -p /mnt  2. Mount the Amazon file cache to the directory that you just created. Use the following command and replace these names: o Replace cache_dns_name with the actual file cache’s Domain Name System (DNS) name o Replace mountname with the cache’s mount name, which you can get by running the describe-file-caches AWS CLI command or DescribeFileCaches API operation sudo mount -t lustre -o relatime,flock cache_dns_name@tcp:/mountname /mnt Note: Make sure your EC2 instance is in the same VPC as your cache. If done correctly, the path of your folder will show up in the /mnt folder. You can also use the df command to see the DNS and mount point is attached to your file system:
justification: While certain environmental checks (such as VPC alignment) can be automated, the core requirement of this control is to verify that the FSx cache is actually mounted on the EC2 instance at the OS level. This state is not visible or auditable via AWS APIs, boto3, AWS CLI, or Config, as mounting is an in-guest operation. Even with AWS Systems Manager, you would still need to run OS-level commands and interpret their output, which is fundamentally a manual audit step. Therefore, full automation is not feasible for this control.
manual_summary: 1. **SSH into the EC2 Instance:** - Use your SSH client and appropriate credentials to access the target EC2 instance. 2. **Verify/Create Mount Directory:** - Run: `sudo mkdir -p /mnt` - Confirm the directory exists for mounting the FSx cache. 3. **Check Mount Status:** - Run: `mount | grep lustre` - Alternatively, run: `df -h | grep /mnt` - Confirm that the FSx cache is mounted at the intended mount point (e.g., `/mnt`) and that the source matches the expected FSx DNS name and mount name. 4. **Validate Against FSx Details:** - Use `aws fsx describe-file-caches` to retrieve the cache DNS name and mount name. - Ensure the mounted file system matches these details. 5. **Document Evidence:** - Take a screenshot or copy the terminal output showing the successful mount and matching details.

source: CIS AWS STORAGE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1
title: Ensure exporting cache to S3
description: The S3 bucket we created earlier will store the files generated at this mount point.
rationale: The rationale behind using the S3 bucket to store files generated at the mount point is to ensure scalable, durable, and cost-effective storage for your data. By exporting files to S3, you benefit from its high availability and robust data management features, which enhances data security and accessibility. This approach also optimizes storage resource utilization and simplifies data backup and retrieval processes.
audit: We can export the files that were created to the S3 bucket using the following steps: 1. Create a file on the FSx mount point: 2. Run the command: sudo touch efx.txt  3. Now run the command: sudo lsm hsm_archive efx.txt  4. Now check your S3 bucket that was created earlier.
remediation: References: 1. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonElastiCache/latest/red-ug/backups- exporting.html
justification: While the verification of the file's presence in the S3 bucket could be automated, the core of this control requires creating a file on an FSx mount point and triggering an export using a command-line tool (`lsm hsm_archive`) that operates at the filesystem level. These steps require direct access to the file system (typically via SSH) and cannot be performed or validated using AWS APIs, boto3, or AWS CLI alone. Therefore, the overall control cannot be fully automated.
manual_summary: 1. **Access the FSx Mount Point:** - SSH into the EC2 instance or system where the FSx file system is mounted. - Navigate to the mounted FSx directory. 2. **Create a Test File:** - Run: `sudo touch efx.txt` to create a test file at the mount point. 3. **Export the File to S3:** - Execute: `sudo lsm hsm_archive efx.txt` to initiate the export of the file to the configured S3 bucket. 4. **Verify Export in S3:** - Log in to the AWS Management Console. - Navigate to the S3 service and open the relevant bucket. - Confirm that the file `efx.txt` appears in the bucket, indicating a successful export. 5. **Document Evidence:** - Take screenshots or export logs showing the file creation, export command execution, and the file's presence in S3.

source: CIS AWS STORAGE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1
title: Ensure Elastic Disaster Recovery is Configured
description: AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery is a service that enables you to create and maintain backups of your workloads on AWS, particularly your servers. This service is crucial for ensuring high resilience for your AWS workloads. It operates by establishing and maintaining backups in selected AWS regions, guaranteeing that your data is safe, durable, and highly available in the event of issues in the primary availability zone or region where your AWS server is located.
rationale: AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery is crucial for establishing high resiliency in the cloud, synonymous with effective disaster recovery. High resiliency measures your organization's ability to respond to and recover from disasters impacting IT infrastructure. Achieving high resiliency minimizes downtime and long-term costs associated with outages, while low resiliency can result in prolonged downtime, potential data loss, and even permanent infrastructure damage.
audit: 1. Review Disaster Recovery Plans : o Log in to the AWS Management Console. o Navigate to the AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery service. o Locate and open the disaster recovery plans. o Verify that the plans are current and comprehensive, covering all critical workloads. o Ensure that the plans specify clear recovery time objectives (RTO) and recovery point objectives (RPO). 2. Check Backup Configurations : o In the AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery dashboard, review the list of protected servers and workloads. o Confirm that backups are enabled for all critical servers and workloads. o Verify the backup schedule and frequency to ensure they meet organizational requirements. o Check that backups are being stored in the correct AWS regions as specified in the disaster recovery plan. 3. Test Recovery Procedures : o Identify a non-production environment to conduct recovery drills. o Initiate a simulated disaster scenario to test the recovery procedures. o Execute the recovery process for each critical workload. o Measure and document the time taken to recover each workload. o Compare the measured recovery times against the RTO and RPO. o Identify and document any issues or delays encountered during the recovery process. 4. Monitor Backup Integrity : o Open the AWS CloudWatch console. o Set up CloudWatch Alarms to monitor the status of backups. o Configure alerts for any failed or incomplete backups. o Regularly review the CloudWatch logs to verify that backups are successfully completed and stored. 5. Evaluate Backup Storage and Security : o Access the AWS S3 or Glacier console, depending on where backups are stored. o Verify that all backup data is encrypted in transit and at rest. o Check the storage settings to confirm that data is being stored in secure, durable storage solutions. o Review the access control policies to ensure that only authorized personnel have access to backup data. 6. Ensure Compliance with Policies and Regulations : o Review organizational and regulatory compliance requirements relevant to disaster recovery. o Ensure that the disaster recovery practices and configurations comply with these requirements. o Document the compliance efforts, including any specific steps taken to meet industry standards and regulations. o Prepare reports or evidence of compliance for any upcoming audits or assessments.
remediation: 1. Update Disaster Recovery Plans : o Action : Log in to the AWS Management Console. o Procedure : ▪ Navigate to the AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery service. ▪ Locate and review the current disaster recovery plans. ▪ Update the plans to ensure they are comprehensive and cover all critical workloads. ▪ Ensure that the plans specify clear recovery time objectives (RTO) and recovery point objectives (RPO). ▪ Save and document the updated plans. 2. Correct Backup Configurations : o Action : Verify and adjust backup settings. o Procedure : ▪ In the AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery dashboard, review the list of protected servers and workloads. ▪ Enable backups for any critical servers and workloads that are not currently being backed up. ▪ Adjust the backup schedule and frequency to meet organizational requirements. ▪ Ensure backups are stored in the correct AWS regions as specified in the disaster recovery plan. 3. Conduct Recovery Procedure Drills : o Action : Test and refine recovery procedures. o Procedure : ▪ Identify a non-production environment to conduct recovery drills. ▪ Simulate a disaster scenario to test the recovery procedures. ▪ Execute the recovery process for each critical workload. ▪ Measure and document the time taken to recover each workload. ▪ Compare the measured recovery times against the RTO and RPO. ▪ Identify and address any issues or delays encountered during the recovery process. ▪ Update the recovery procedures based on the findings from the drill. 4. Ensure Backup Integrity : o Action : Monitor and verify the integrity of backups. o Procedure : ▪ Open the AWS CloudWatch console. ▪ Set up CloudWatch Alarms to monitor the status of backups. ▪ Configure alerts for any failed or incomplete backups. ▪ Regularly review CloudWatch logs to verify that backups are successfully completed and stored. ▪ Resolve any issues identified in the logs, such as incomplete or failed backups. 5. Enhance Backup Storage and Security : o Action : Improve the storage and security of backup data. o Procedure : ▪ Access the AWS S3 or Glacier console, depending on where backups are stored. ▪ Ensure all backup data is encrypted in transit and at rest. ▪ Adjust storage settings to confirm that data is being stored in secure, durable storage solutions. ▪ Review and update access control policies to ensure only authorized personnel can access backup data. ▪ Implement any additional security measures necessary to protect the backup data. 6. Ensure Compliance with Policies and Regulations : o Action : Align disaster recovery practices with compliance requirements. o Procedure : ▪ Review organizational and regulatory compliance requirements relevant to disaster recovery. ▪ Adjust disaster recovery practices and configurations to ensure compliance with these requirements. ▪ Document the compliance efforts, including specific steps taken to meet industry standards and regulations. ▪ Prepare and maintain reports or evidence of compliance for any upcoming audits or assessments.
justification: While some aspects of AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery configuration (such as verifying protected instances and snapshot status) can be automated via AWS APIs (e.g., boto3's `describe_recovery_instances`), the core of this control requires reviewing the actual disaster recovery plans for comprehensiveness, currency, and alignment with business objectives (RTO/RPO). These plans are typically documented outside of AWS resources and require human judgment to assess adequacy, coverage, and compliance with organizational policies—none of which are fully exposed or verifiable via AWS APIs.
manual_summary: 1. Log in to the AWS Management Console. 2. Navigate to the AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery service. 3. Locate and open the disaster recovery plans. 4. Review each plan to ensure: - All critical workloads are included. - The plan is current (recently reviewed/updated). - Recovery Time Objectives (RTO) and Recovery Point Objectives (RPO) are clearly specified and appropriate. - The plan is comprehensive, covering all necessary steps for disaster recovery. 5. Confirm that backup configurations for all protected servers are present and up to date. 6. Document findings, including any gaps or outdated plans, and ensure remediation steps are tracked.

source: CIS AWS STORAGE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1
title: Ensure AWS Disaster Recovery Configuration
description: It’s important to understand how the network on EDR works. This isn’t a simple service to configure, but it works with multiple work loads over the network. You can connect your on-premises or third-party cloud service to AWS EDR over the network. Below are the descriptions of the AWS network architecture: 1. Your local network inside the data center or cloud a.Connect an AWS Replication Agent to each of your resources. 2. AWS Cloud Architecture a. Choose the AWS Region that you want to house your disaster recovery instances. b.Create AWS API Endpoints for EC2, Disaster Recovery, and S3. c.Upon creation of Disaster Recovery endpoints, two subnets will be created in your VPC i.Staging Area Subnets: Replication servers with EBS volumes attached to each disk on the replication servers. ii.Recovery Subnets: Recovery EC2 instances attached to EBS volumes/ d.Connect local network over TCP port 443 to EDR and S3 e.Connect local replication agent to AWS replication servers over TCP port 1500 f.Connectivity out of staging area: Connect staging area on AWS to EDR over TCP port 443 g.Allow connection to S3 over TCP 443 h.Allow connectivity to EC2 over TCP 443 to connect to API Endpoint
remediation: References: 1. https://aws.amazon.com/disaster-recovery/
justification: While some AWS-side disaster recovery configurations (such as VPC, subnet, and endpoint existence) can be partially verified with automation, the core of this control requires validating the end-to-end disaster recovery architecture, including on-premises replication agents, network connectivity, and cross-cloud integrations. These elements are not fully exposed or verifiable via AWS APIs, as they involve external systems and manual network configuration checks. Therefore, a manual approach is necessary to ensure comprehensive compliance.
manual_summary: 1. **Review Disaster Recovery Architecture Documentation:** - Obtain and review the organization's disaster recovery (DR) architecture diagrams and documentation. - Confirm that the architecture includes both AWS and on-premises/cloud components as described in the control. 2. **Verify On-Premises Replication Agent Installation:** - Confirm that AWS Replication Agents are installed and running on all required on-premises or third-party cloud resources. - Check agent logs or management consoles to ensure successful connectivity to AWS. 3. **Validate AWS Network Configuration:** - In the AWS Console, navigate to VPC and EC2 dashboards. - Confirm the existence of required subnets (e.g., Staging Area Subnets, Recovery Subnets) and that Replication Servers are deployed in the correct subnets. - Verify security group and network ACL configurations to ensure proper communication between on-premises and AWS resources. 4. **Check Cross-Cloud/On-Premises Connectivity:** - Confirm that network connectivity (VPN, Direct Connect, or other secure tunnels) is established and functioning between on-premises/cloud resources and AWS. - Test connectivity using network tools (e.g., ping, traceroute) from both sides. 5. **Review Failover and Recovery Procedures:** - Ensure documented and tested procedures exist for failover and recovery, including regular DR drills. - Review evidence of recent DR tests and their outcomes. 6. **Confirm Compliance:** - All above elements must be present, documented, and functioning as intended for the environment to be compliant.

source: CIS AWS STORAGE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1
title: Ensure functionality of Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR)
description: Establish and maintain an effective Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) system to proactively monitor, detect, and respond to security threats on endpoints such as computers, mobile devices, and servers. This involves deploying EDR software that continuously collects data from endpoints, analyzes this data for signs of malicious activity, and provides real-time alerts and detailed incident reports. Regularly test and update the EDR system to ensure it can accurately identify and mitigate advanced threats, including zero-day exploits and sophisticated malware, ensuring comprehensive protection and swift response to potential security incidents.
rationale: Ensuring the functionality of Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) systems is essential for early detection and swift response to security threats on endpoints. These systems continuously monitor and analyze endpoint data, providing real-time alerts and detailed incident reports to identify and mitigate potential threats. Regular testing and updates of the EDR system ensure it remains effective against advanced threats, maintaining comprehensive protection for the organization's assets.
audit: 1. Preparing the Environment for EDR - Before getting started with EDR, you must prepare the environment that you want to back up. 2. Preparing the Source Server - Allow direct access to Elastic Disaster Recovery and Amazon S3 AWS service API endpoints through HTTPS protocol (TCP port 443). Direct outbound TCP port 1500 from the source server to the staging area subnet, which contains the replication servers. 3. Preparing the Staging Area Subnet - Allow Direct access to EDR, S3, and EC2 through HTTPS protocol (TCP port 443) Direct inbound TCP port 1500 for replication traffic 4. Accessing the AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery Console - o Search for “AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery” in the AWS Console. o Select “Elastic Disaster Recovery” 5. Configuring the Replication Settings Template - Select Configure and Initializein in the AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery screen. You will be navigated to setup your replication settings template. This will create a staging area in a subnet of your choice and a replication server instance types. The default replication server instance type will be a t3 micro EC2 instance. This is good for normal workloads with small I/O operations. 6. Next, configure EBS encryption and volume types. This will depend on your workload requirements. 7. To encrypt EBS volumes, leave the setting as “default.” If you wish to make a custom encryption setting, you will need to create an AWS KMS key. 8. Configure the security group to your specific needs. Remember what ports need to be opened on inbound / outbound traffic that was specified in previous steps: You can choose how you want your data routed and if you want to throttle network traffic to reserve bandwidth. To keep your data as secure as possible, it’s recommended to get set up with a VPN or AWS direct connect, so your backups are not traveling over the public internet. Point in time policy defines the snapshot retention time. Because Elastic Disaster Recovery service uses incremental backups, it’s not necessary to keep old copies of backups. Now, you’re ready to launch this template.
justification: The core requirement of this control is to ensure the deployment, configuration, and ongoing functionality of Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) systems on endpoints, which are typically outside the direct purview of AWS-native APIs. While some integration points (such as forwarding EDR alerts to AWS Security Hub or CloudWatch) could be checked via automation, the primary audit objective—verifying EDR presence, correct configuration, and operational effectiveness on all relevant endpoints—cannot be achieved solely through AWS APIs or automation. This requires manual inspection of EDR consoles, endpoint agents, and policy enforcement.
manual_summary: 1. **Verify EDR Deployment on Endpoints:** - Access the management console of your EDR provider (e.g., CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, etc.). - Confirm that EDR agents are installed and actively running on all relevant endpoints (servers, workstations, and any AWS EC2 instances in scope). 2. **Check EDR Policy Enforcement:** - Review the EDR console to ensure security policies for threat detection, response, and alerting are defined and enforced across all endpoints. 3. **Test EDR Functionality:** - Simulate a benign threat (e.g., EICAR test file) on a test endpoint to confirm the EDR system detects and responds as expected. - Review incident logs and alerts generated by the EDR system for completeness and accuracy. 4. **Review Integration with AWS (if applicable):** - If EDR is configured to forward alerts to AWS Security Hub or CloudWatch, manually verify that alerts are being received in those AWS services. 5. **Document Findings:** - Record evidence (screenshots, logs, policy exports) demonstrating EDR deployment, policy enforcement, and alerting functionality.

source: CIS AWS STORAGE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1
title: Ensure configuration of replication settings
description: Set up and maintain the replication settings to ensure accurate and efficient data duplication across systems. Proper configuration includes specifying source and target locations, defining replication schedules, and setting bandwidth limits to optimize performance. Regularly review and update these settings to accommodate changes in data volume and network conditions, ensuring data integrity and availability during replication processes.
rationale: Proper configuration of replication settings is essential to ensure data consistency and availability across systems. Accurate replication schedules and bandwidth management optimize performance and prevent network congestion. Regular reviews and updates of these settings help adapt to changes in data volume and network conditions, maintaining efficient and reliable data replication processes.
audit: 1. Select “Configure and Initialize” in in the AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery screen. You will be navigated to setup your replication settings template. This will create a staging area in a subnet of your choice and a replication server instance types. The default replication server instance type will be a t3 micro EC2 instance. This is good for normal workloads with small I/O operations. 2. Next, configure EBS encryption and volume types. This will depend on your workload requirements. To encrypt EBS volumes, leave the setting as “default.” If you wish to make a custom encryption setting, you will need to create an AWS KMS key. 3. Configure the security group to your specific needs. Remember what ports need to be opened on inbound / outbound traffic that was specified in previous steps: o Configure Additional Replication settings. o You can choose how you want your data routed and if you want to throttle network traffic to reserve bandwidth. To keep your data as secure as possible, it’s recommended to get set up with a VPN or AWS direct connect, so your backups are not traveling over the public internet. o Point in time policy defines the snapshot retention time. Because Elastic Disaster Recovery service uses incremental backups, it’s not necessary to keep old copies of backups. Now, you’re ready to launch this template.
justification: While some aspects of AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery replication settings (such as EBS encryption and instance types) can be queried via AWS APIs, the full scope of this control—specifically the initial configuration, selection of source/target, bandwidth limits, and schedule settings—requires interactive, context-driven decisions that are not fully exposed or auditable via AWS APIs or Config. The audit steps explicitly reference manual actions in the AWS Console, and critical settings (like bandwidth throttling and replication schedules) are not programmatically retrievable or verifiable in a comprehensive, compliance-focused manner.
manual_summary: 1. Log in to the AWS Management Console and navigate to the AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery service. 2. Select “Configure and Initialize” for the relevant source server. 3. In the replication settings template, verify: - The correct source and target locations are specified. - The appropriate subnet and replication server instance type are selected (default is t3.micro; verify suitability for workload). - Replication schedule and bandwidth limits are configured according to organizational requirements. - EBS encryption and volume types are set per workload and compliance needs. 4. Review all settings for alignment with current data volume, network conditions, and business continuity requirements. 5. Document the configuration and any changes made. 6. Repeat this review regularly or after significant infrastructure changes.

source: CIS AWS STORAGE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1
title: Ensure installation of the AWS Replication Agent
description: Set up and verify the installation of the AWS Replication Agent on all relevant systems to facilitate efficient and reliable data replication. This process includes downloading the agent, configuring it according to best practices, and ensuring it is correctly integrated with your AWS environment. Regularly check the agent’s performance and update it as needed to maintain optimal functionality and data integrity during replication processes.
rationale: Installing the AWS Replication Agent is crucial for enabling efficient and reliable data replication, ensuring that critical data is accurately duplicated across systems. Proper configuration and integration with your AWS environment optimize the agent’s performance, enhancing data availability and disaster recovery capabilities. Regular checks and updates of the replication agent help maintain its effectiveness, ensuring data integrity and minimizing the risk of replication failures.
audit: 1. On the source servers page, from Actions, choose add servers to obtain the agent installer link. 2. On your source server (in our case, the EC2 instance that was already created) download the appropriate agent installer for your operating system. o For Linux instance on US-East-1. Substitute your region in the {Region} brackets of this command: wget -O ./aws-replication-installer-init https://aws-elastic-disaster- recovery-us-east-1.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/latest/linux/aws-replication- installer-init  3. Run following command: chmod +x aws-replication-installer-init; sudo ./aws-replication-installer- init  4. Type in your region. Region is case sensitive: if you’re in us-east-1, make sure you type “us-east-1”. 5. If you’re using SSH, you will be prompted with your activation ID and secret activation key. Make sure you have those accessible for the IAM user you’re using. You can generate a new key from the IAM dashboard if you forgot to save your key. 6. Select “Enter” to replicate all servers. 7. All servers should replicate. 8. Make sure your OS is up to date. If you run into an error replicating your devices, view the documentation on troubleshooting the AWS replication installation here: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/mgn/latest/ug/installation-requirements.html. 9. If install runs successfully, the source server will appear in your Elastic Disaster Recovery Console dashboard on the “source servers” page. This will signify the beginning of the replication process.
remediation: References: 1. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/mgn/latest/ug/agent-installation.html
justification: While the presence and status of source servers can be queried via AWS APIs (e.g., using boto3's describe_source_servers for Elastic Disaster Recovery), the actual installation of the AWS Replication Agent on the OS level (e.g., via SSH into EC2, downloading, and running the installer) cannot be automated or verified solely through AWS APIs. The agent's installation process requires direct interaction with the server's operating system, which is outside the scope of AWS API visibility. Therefore, full compliance with this control requires manual steps.
manual_summary: 1. **Access the Source Server:** - SSH into each relevant EC2 instance (or other source server) where the AWS Replication Agent must be installed. 2. **Download and Install the AWS Replication Agent:** - In the AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery Console, navigate to "Source servers" and select "Add servers" to obtain the correct installer link for your region. - On the server, run the provided wget command to download the installer (e.g., `wget -O ./aws-replication-installer-init https://aws-elastic-disaster-recovery-<region>.s3.<region>.amazonaws.com/latest/linux/aws-replication-installer-init`). - Make the installer executable (`chmod +x aws-replication-installer-init`) and run it (`sudo ./aws-replication-installer-init`). 3. **Configure and Complete Installation:** - Follow the on-screen prompts to complete the installation, specifying the AWS region and any required activation information. 4. **Verify Installation:** - Confirm that the agent is running on the server (e.g., check running processes or systemd services). - Optionally, verify in the AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery Console that the server appears as a registered source server. 5. **Document Compliance:** - Record the date, server name, and installer version for audit purposes.

source: CIS AWS STORAGE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1
title: Ensure Continuous Disaster Recovery Operations
